FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data with 3498 adolescents aged 9-11 years from baseline, Year 1, and Year 2 assessments

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific screen time variable from ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey reporting average daily hours spent on TV, YouTube, video games, texting, video chatting, and social media

FT_IC3.evidence: Cox proportional hazards regression models examining prospective associations of screen time (continuous and categorical) with transition from overweight/obesity to normal BMI percentile

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric values reported: screen time continuous HR: 0.99 (95% CI 0.96, 1.02) overall; HR: 1.00 (95% CI 0.95, 1.05) females; HR: 0.99 (95% CI 0.95, 1.02) males

FT_IC5.evidence: Longitudinal empirical analysis using Cox proportional hazards regression models with median 657 days follow-up

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in International Journal of Obesity, 2025

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is the independent variable (exposure), not the dependent variable. Paper examines associations of screen time with BMI transition (BMI is outcome). FT-EC7 applies only when screen time is the dependent variable predicted by demographics.
notes: Paper reports null association between screen time and BMI transition likelihood. Despite non-significant findings, all inclusion criteria are met. Study design is prospective longitudinal with screen time as predictor of BMI outcome.
